behaviors.description: A cell containing =SUM(A1:A10) evaluates to the correct sum of the values in cells A1 through A10.
behaviors.justification: The behavior requires range parsing (A1:A10 → cell references), cell lookup, and summation to work together. A Unit test with mocked cell values would miss failures in range resolution logic — for example, if A1:A10 is incorrectly parsed as A1:A9, a mocked array [1,2,...,10] would still pass. Integration testing with an actual cell grid catches range-to-cells mapping errors.
behaviors.test_description: Create a spreadsheet with cells A1–A10 containing values 1–10, enter formula =SUM(A1:A10) in cell A11, evaluate, and assert the result is 55. Repeat with different ranges (e.g., A1:A5, A3:A8) to verify range boundaries are honored.

behaviors.description: A cell referencing another cell that contains a formula (e.g., =B1+1 where B1 is =A1*2) produces the correct cascaded result.
behaviors.justification: Cascaded formulas require the engine to resolve dependencies, evaluate B1 before C1, cache intermediate results, and propagate values correctly. This involves multiple cells, multiple formulas, and dependency ordering. A Unit test would require mocking the dependency graph and intermediate results, which could hide failures in how dependencies are discovered or evaluated in the wrong order. Integration testing ensures the dependency resolution and evaluation order are correct.
behaviors.test_description: Create cells: A1=5, B1=formula(=A1*2), C1=formula(=B1+1). Evaluate the spreadsheet and assert C1 evaluates to 11. Test with longer chains (A→B→C→D) and verify that changing A updates D correctly without re-evaluating B or C prematurely.

behaviors.description: The engine detects a circular dependency (A1 references B1, B1 references A1) and returns a circular reference error instead of looping.
behaviors.justification: While the cycle-detection algorithm itself could be Unit tested in isolation (given a graph with a known cycle, detect it), the real failure boundary is: formulas form a cycle, and the engine must not hang or crash. If you mock the dependency graph builder, you might miss failures where cycles are not correctly identified during graph construction from actual formulas. Integration testing ensures the full pipeline—formula parsing → dependency discovery → cycle detection—works correctly.
behaviors.test_description: Create cells A1 and B1, set A1=formula(=B1), set B1=formula(=A1), and attempt to evaluate. Assert that the engine returns a circular-reference error (not an infinite loop timeout) and does not hang. Test with longer cycles (A→B→C→A) and mixed direct/indirect cycles.

behaviors.description: VLOOKUP with an exact match returns the correct value from the lookup table, and returns an error when the key is not found.
behaviors.justification: VLOOKUP requires parsing the lookup-table range (e.g., A1:B10), determining which column to return, and performing an accurate search. A Unit test with a mocked lookup table could pass even if range parsing is broken or the search algorithm has off-by-one errors. Integration testing with actual cells in a lookup table ensures range resolution, table structure parsing, and search correctness are all validated together.
behaviors.test_description: Create a lookup table (e.g., A1:B5 with names and prices). In a separate cell, enter VLOOKUP formulas: exact match (key exists), exact match (key absent), exact match on edge values (first/last row). Assert correct values are returned or #N/A error is raised appropriately. Test with multi-column tables and different return-column indices.

behaviors.description: The IF function correctly handles nested conditions (e.g., =IF(A1>10, IF(A1>20, "high", "medium"), "low")).
behaviors.justification: The IF function's logic—evaluating a condition, branching to true or false value—is self-contained and can be tested by providing different cell values and verifying the correct branch is taken. The real failures (wrong condition evaluated, wrong branch returned, type coercion in comparison) can be caught by Unit testing with mocked cell values (A1=5, A1=15, A1=25) without needing a full spreadsheet. Cell-reference resolution is a separate concern.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test the IF function by invoking it with mocked or direct condition values: IF(true, "yes", "no") → "yes"; IF(false, "yes", "no") → "no". For nested conditions, test IF(5>10, IF(5>20, "high", "medium"), "low") → "low"; IF(15>10, IF(15>20, "high", "medium"), "low") → "medium"; IF(25>10, IF(25>20, "high", "medium"), "low") → "high". Verify type coercion and edge cases (null, empty string, zero).

behaviors.description: CONCATENATE joins cell values with the correct separator and handles empty cells by skipping them rather than inserting blank strings.
behaviors.justification: CONCATENATE's behavior depends on how empty cells are detected and resolved to empty strings or null values. A Unit test with mocked empty strings could pass even if the engine incorrectly resolves empty cells (e.g., treats them as the string "" instead of skipping them). Integration testing ensures that actual empty cells in a spreadsheet are correctly identified and skipped, not inserted as blank strings in the output.
behaviors.test_description: Create cells: A1="x", B1=(empty), C1="y". Enter formula =CONCATENATE(A1, "-", B1, "-", C1) and assert result is "x--y" OR "x-y" depending on implementation (if empty cells are skipped, "x-y"; if they are blank strings, "x--y"). Test with all empty, some empty, and no empty cells. Verify that null/uninitialized cells are handled consistently.

behaviors.description: A formula referencing a cell in a different sheet (e.g., =Sheet2!A1+1) resolves correctly when the referenced sheet exists.
behaviors.justification: Cross-sheet references require the engine to parse the sheet-qualified reference (Sheet2!A1), look up the sheet by name, and retrieve the cell value from that sheet. A Unit test with a mocked sheet lookup could pass even if sheet resolution fails. Integration testing with multiple actual sheets ensures the full reference resolution works end-to-end.
behaviors.test_description: Create two sheets: Sheet1 and Sheet2. In Sheet2, set cell A1=10. In Sheet1, create a formula =Sheet2!A1+1 and assert the result is 11. Test error cases: reference to non-existent sheet (Sheet3!A1 → #REF!), reference to existing sheet with non-existent cell. Verify that renaming sheets updates references correctly if that feature is supported.

behaviors.description: When a referenced cell's value changes, all dependent formulas are recalculated in the correct topological order.
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires building a dependency graph from all formulas, detecting which cells depend on the changed cell, performing a topological sort, and recalculating in the correct order. A Unit test of a topological sort algorithm alone would miss failures in dependency discovery or invalidation logic. Integration testing with multiple cells and formulas ensures the full pipeline—change detection, graph traversal, recalculation order—is correct.
behaviors.test_description: Create a formula chain: A1=1, B1=A1*2, C1=B1+5, D1=A1+C1. Change A1 to 2 and assert B1, C1, D1 are recalculated immediately and correctly (B1=4, C1=9, D1=11). Test that recalculation happens exactly once per cell (no redundant calculations). Use a formula that would cause infinite recursion if evaluated out of order (e.g., D1 depends on C1, C1 depends on B1, B1 depends on A1) and verify recalculation completes in linear time.

behaviors.description: The undo operation after editing a formula restores both the formula text and all previously computed dependent values.
behaviors.justification: Undo requires the engine to store the previous state (formula text, formula result, and all dependent values), then restore them on undo. This involves multiple modules: formula storage, state snapshots, and undo-stack management working together. While the undo logic could theoretically be Unit tested with mocked state storage, real failures (incomplete snapshots, corrupted formula text during restore, dependent values not restored) are best caught by Integration testing with actual formula edits and undo operations.
behaviors.test_description: Create a formula chain: A1=5, B1=A1*2 (result 10). Edit B1 to =A1*3 (result 15). Trigger undo and assert B1 reverts to =A1*2 with value 10. Test multiple undos in sequence. Test undo after multiple edits in different cells (A1, B1, C1) to ensure each cell's state is restored independently. Verify that redo also works and re-applies the formula with correct dependent values.

behaviors.description: Saving a workbook to disk and loading it back produces identical cell values and formulas — no data loss or precision change in floating-point results.
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires actual I/O: writing to disk in a binary format and reading back. A Unit test of the serialization logic with mocked file I/O could pass even if the file format is corrupted, endianness is wrong, or floating-point precision is lost during encoding. Only System-level testing—writing to an actual file, reading it back, and comparing byte-for-byte—can catch real failures in persistence. Floating-point precision must be verified against actual binary format choices.
behaviors.test_description: Create a workbook with various cell types: integers, floats (including edge cases like π, 1/3), strings, formulas (=A1+0.1), empty cells. Save to disk in the custom binary format. Load the file in a fresh engine instance and assert all cells match: values are bit-identical (for floats), formulas are textually identical, and empty cells remain empty. Test with large workbooks (1000+ cells) and verify file size is reasonable. Corrupted-file recovery: intentionally truncate the file and verify the engine returns a meaningful error (not silent data loss).
